Topic: 18.1 Periodicity
Question: How do alkali metals differ from alkaline earth metals in atomic structure and general properties?
Answer: The alkali metals all have a single s electron in their outermost shell. In contrast, the alkaline earth metals have a completed s subshell in their outermost shell. In general, the alkali metals react faster and are more reactive than the corresponding alkaline earth metals in the same period.
o3 Answer:
Alkali metals (Group-1: Li, Na, K, Rb, Cs, Fr) and alkaline-earth metals (Group-2: Be, Mg, Ca, Sr, Ba, Ra) lie in successive vertical columns of the s-block.  Their differences spring mainly from the fact that alkali-metal atoms contain one ns electron while alkaline-earth atoms contain two ns electrons.

1. Atomic-structure differences  
   • Valence-shell configuration  
     Alkali: [Noble-gas] ns¹  
     Alkaline earth: [Noble-gas] ns²  

   • Valence electrons  
     Alkali: 1; can lose only one electron.  
     Alkaline earth: 2; can lose two electrons.  

   • Common oxidation state / ion formed  
     Alkali: +1 (M⁺)  
     Alkaline earth: +2 (M²⁺)  

   • First ionisation energy  
     Lower for alkali metals; rises markedly on going to Group 2.  
     (However, Group-2 shows a much larger jump between the 1st and 2nd I.E.)  

   • Atomic and ionic radii (same period)  
     Alkali > Alkaline earth, because the extra proton in Group-2 pulls the electrons in more strongly.  

2. Resulting general physical properties  
   • Hardness and mechanical strength  
     Alkali: very soft, can be cut with a knife.  
     Alkaline earth: harder and more brittle.  

   • Melting / boiling points  
     Much lower for alkali metals; considerably higher for alkaline earth metals.  

   • Densities  
     Alkali metals are the lightest (Li, Na, K float on water).  
     Alkaline earth metals have higher densities.  

3. General chemical behaviour  
   • Reactivity toward oxygen, water, acids  
     Alkali metals react violently with cold water, form strong, highly soluble hydroxides (MOH).  
     Alkaline earth metals react less vigorously; some (Be, Mg) need steam or hot water; hydroxides (M(OH)₂) are less soluble and less basic (except Ba(OH)₂).  

   • Nature of compounds  
     Alkali compounds contain M⁺; they are highly ionic, highly soluble; carbonates, nitrates, chlorides are heat-stable.  
     Alkaline earth compounds contain M²⁺; higher lattice and hydration energies give many sparingly-soluble salts (carbonates, sulphates, phosphates) which decompose on heating (e.g. CaCO₃ → CaO + CO₂).  

   • Reducing power  
     Alkali metals are stronger reducing agents (M → M⁺ + e⁻, lower IE).  
     Alkaline earth metals are less powerful reducers.  

   • Oxides  
     Alkali metals give normal oxides (Li₂O), peroxides (Na₂O₂) or superoxides (KO₂, RbO₂, CsO₂).  
     Alkaline earth metals give only normal oxides, MO.  

   • Flame colours (due to ns → np excitation)  
     Alkali: Li-crimson, Na-yellow, K-lilac, etc.  
     Alkaline earth: Ca-brick red, Sr-crimson, Ba-apple-green; Be and Mg give no persistent flame colour.  

In short, the presence of one loosely held ns¹ electron makes alkali metals larger, softer, with lower melting points, much lower ionisation energy, and therefore far more reactive and strongly basic (+1) chemistry.  The two ns electrons of alkaline-earth metals are held more strongly, so these elements are smaller, harder, less reactive, and show characteristic +2 chemistry with less soluble, less basic compounds.
o4-mini Answer:
Alkali metals (Group 1: Li, Na, K, Rb, Cs, Fr) and alkaline‐earth metals (Group 2: Be, Mg, Ca, Sr, Ba, Ra) lie side by side in the periodic table, but they differ in both their atomic structures and their chemical and physical properties.  

1. Atomic structure  
  • Valence‐electron configuration  
    – Alkali metals: ns¹ (one electron in the outer s orbital)  
    – Alkaline-earth metals: ns² (two electrons in the outer s orbital)  
  • Effective nuclear charge and atomic radius  
    – Having only one valence electron, Group 1 atoms “shield” that electron more completely, so they have lower effective nuclear charge per valence electron and therefore larger atomic radii than the Group 2 atoms in the same period.  
    – Group 2 atoms, with one more proton but “similar” shielding, pull their valence electrons in more tightly, giving smaller radii.  
  • Ionization energies  
    – First ionization energy (IE₁) rises going from Group 1 to Group 2 in the same period, because the second-group atom holds its ns² electrons more tightly.  
    – Group 1 metals have the lowest IE’s in their periods, which is why they are extremely reducing.  

2. General physical properties  
  • Softness and density  
    – Alkali metals are very soft (you can cut them with a knife), have low densities (Li, Na, K float on water).  
    – Alkaline-earth metals are harder and denser.  
  • Melting and boiling points  
    – Group 2 metals generally have higher melting and boiling points, reflecting stronger metallic bonding (more delocalized electrons per atom).  
  • Color in flame tests  
    – Alkali metals give intense, characteristic flame colors (Li red, Na yellow, K lilac, etc.).  
    – Alkaline-earth metals also color flames but often less brilliantly (Ca orange-red, Sr crimson, Ba green).  

3. Chemical reactivity  
  • Oxidation states  
    – Alkali metals form only +1 ions (M⁺).  
    – Alkaline-earth metals form only +2 ions (M²⁺).  
  • Reaction with water  
    – Group 1: M(s) + H₂O(l) → MOH(aq) + ½ H₂(g) is vigorous—even explosive with K, Rb, Cs.  
    – Group 2: M(s) + 2 H₂O(l) → M(OH)₂(aq) + H₂(g) is less vigorous; often requires heating (Mg reacts very slowly at room temperature; Ca, Sr, Ba react more readily but still less violently than Na).  
  • Acidity/basicity of hydroxides  
    – Alkali-metal hydroxides (MOH) are strong bases and very soluble.  
    – Alkaline-earth hydroxides (M(OH)₂) are also strong bases but less soluble (e.g. Mg(OH)₂ is sparingly soluble).  

4. Trends down the group  
  • Both groups become more reactive downward, but the effect is stronger in Group 1 because each successive alkali metal sees a larger increase in atomic size and drop in IE.  
  • Densities, melting points, and reactivities all increase more steeply down Group 2 than down Group 1, but Group 2 remains overall less reactive.  

In summary, alkali metals (ns¹) are larger, softer, more weakly held to their valence electron, and more violently reactive than the slightly harder, more tightly bound ns² alkaline-earth metals, which form doubly charged cations and show higher melting points and ionization energies.

- Alkaline earth metals form **divalent cations (M²⁺)**, while alkali metals form **monovalent cations (M⁺)**.

gpt-5 Answer:
Key differences between alkali metals (Group 1) and alkaline earth metals (Group 2):

Atomic structure
- Valence configuration: Alkali metals have ns1; alkaline earth metals have ns2.
- Common oxidation state: Alkali form M+; alkaline earth form M2+.
- Ionization energies: Alkali have lower first ionization energies; their second IE is very high (so they stay +1). Alkaline earth have higher first IE but comparatively accessible second IE, enabling +2.
- Size and charge density: For the same period, alkali atoms and M+ ions are larger; M2+ of alkaline earth are smaller, with higher charge density and higher hydration enthalpy.
- Metallic bonding: Weaker in alkali (one valence electron) than in alkaline earth (two), affecting hardness and melting points.

General properties
- Reactivity: Both are reactive metals; alkali are more reactive overall (vigorous with cold water). Alkaline earth are less reactive; Be does not react with water, Mg reacts with steam, Ca/Sr/Ba react with cold water less vigorously than alkali.
- Physical properties: Alkali are softer, have lower melting/boiling points, and lower densities; alkaline earth are harder, denser, and have higher melting points.
- Basic oxides/hydroxides: Alkali oxides/hydroxides are strongly basic and generally very soluble. Alkaline earth oxides/hydroxides are less soluble; basicity increases down the group (Be(OH)2 amphoteric, Mg(OH)2 weakly basic, Ba(OH)2 strongly basic).
- Typical salts and solubility: Alkali salts (e.g., halides, nitrates, carbonates) are usually soluble; stable solid bicarbonates exist. Alkaline earth carbonates and phosphates are largely insoluble; sulfates decrease in solubility down the group; solid bicarbonates do not exist (only in solution).
- Special compounds: Heavier alkali form peroxides/superoxides readily; alkaline earth mainly form normal oxides (Ba and Sr can form peroxides, but superoxides are rare). Alkaline earth (except Be) form nitrides directly; among alkali, mainly Li forms a nitride.
- Bonding character: Alkali compounds are mostly ionic. Alkaline earth compounds are also ionic, but Be2+ (and to a lesser extent Mg2+) has high polarizing power, giving more covalent character and complex formation (e.g., BeCl2 covalent, amphoterism of Be compounds).
- Reducing power: Both are strong reducing agents; alkali are generally stronger (more negative standard reduction potentials).
- Flame tests: Alkali give intense flame colors easily; alkaline earth require stronger heating; Be and Mg give no flame color.

Answer: $$\text{Na} + \left(\text{I}\right)_{2} \rightarrow \text{2NaI} \\ \text{2Na} + \text{Se} \rightarrow \left(\text{Na}\right)_{2} \text{Se} \\ \text{2Na} + \left(\text{O}\right)_{2} \rightarrow \left(\text{Na}\right)_{2} \left(\text{O}\right)_{2}$$ $$\text{Sr} + \left(\text{I}\right)_{2} \rightarrow \left(\text{SrI}\right)_{2} \\ \text{Sr} + \text{Se} \rightarrow \text{SrSe} \\ \text{2Sr} + \left(\text{O}\right)_{2} \rightarrow \text{2SrO}$$ $$\text{2Al} + \left(\text{3I}\right)_{2} \rightarrow \left(\text{2AlI}\right)_{3} \\ \text{2Al} + \text{3Se} \rightarrow \left(\text{Al}\right)_{2} \left(\text{Se}\right)_{3} \\ \text{4Al} + \left(\text{3O}\right)_{2} \rightarrow \left(\text{2Al}\right)_{2} \left(\text{O}\right)_{3}$$
o3 Answer:
Binary ionic compounds are written so the total positive and negative charges cancel.  
For the metallic elements in column 1 use their common ionic charges

• Na → Na⁺  
• Sr → Sr²⁺  
• Al → Al³⁺  

For the non-metals in column 2 use their common ionic charges  

• I → I⁻  
• Se → Se²⁻  
• O → O²⁻  

Combine cations and anions in the smallest whole-number ratio that gives overall charge 0.

1. Compounds of Na⁺  
   Na⁺ + I⁻  → NaI  
   2 Na⁺ + Se²⁻ → Na₂Se  
   2 Na⁺ + O²⁻  → Na₂O  

2. Compounds of Sr²⁺  
   Sr²⁺ + 2 I⁻  → SrI₂  
   Sr²⁺ + Se²⁻  → SrSe  
   Sr²⁺ + O²⁻   → SrO  

3. Compounds of Al³⁺  
   Al³⁺ + 3 I⁻  → AlI₃  
   2 Al³⁺ + 3 Se²⁻ → Al₂Se₃  
   2 Al³⁺ + 3 O²⁻  → Al₂O₃  

Thus the nine predicted formulas are:

NaI, Na₂Se, Na₂O,  
SrI₂, SrSe, SrO,  
AlI₃, Al₂Se₃, Al₂O₃.

gemini-2.5-pro Answer:
Of course! Here is the solution to the problem.

### **Underlying Principle**

To predict the formulas of the compounds, we need to determine the common ionic charges for each element. The elements in column 1 are metals, which will lose electrons to form positive ions (cations). The elements in column 2 are non-metals, which will gain electrons to form negative ions (anions). The final compound must be electrically neutral, meaning the total positive charge must balance the total negative charge.

### **Step 1: Determine the Ionic Charges**

**Column 1 (Cations):**
*   **Na (Sodium):** In Group 1 of the periodic table, it loses one electron to form **Na⁺**.
*   **Sr (Strontium):** In Group 2, it loses two electrons to form **Sr²⁺**.
*   **Al (Aluminum):** In Group 13, it loses three electrons to form **Al³⁺**.

**Column 2 (Anions):**
*   **I (Iodine):** In Group 17 (halogens), it gains one electron to form **I⁻**.
*   **Se (Selenium):** In Group 16, it gains two electrons to form **Se²⁻**.
*   **O (Oxygen):** In Group 16, it gains two electrons to form **O²⁻**.

### **Step 2: Combine Ions to Form Neutral Compounds**

We will combine each cation with each anion, ensuring the resulting formula has a net charge of zero. A simple way to do this is the "criss-cross" method, where the magnitude of the charge on one ion becomes the subscript for the other ion. Remember to simplify the subscripts to the lowest whole-number ratio.

**1. Reactions with Sodium (Na⁺)**
*   **Na⁺ + I⁻ → NaI** (The +1 and -1 charges balance)
*   **Na⁺ + Se²⁻ → Na₂Se** (Two Na⁺ ions are needed to balance one Se²⁻ ion)
*   **Na⁺ + O²⁻ → Na₂O** (Two Na⁺ ions are needed to balance one O²⁻ ion)

**2. Reactions with Strontium (Sr²⁺)**
*   **Sr²⁺ + I⁻ → SrI₂** (Two I⁻ ions are needed to balance one Sr²⁺ ion)
*   **Sr²⁺ + Se²⁻ → SrSe** (The +2 and -2 charges balance. Subscripts of 2 and 2 simplify to 1 and 1)
*   **Sr²⁺ + O²⁻ → SrO** (The +2 and -2 charges balance)

**3. Reactions with Aluminum (Al³⁺)**
*   **Al³⁺ + I⁻ → AlI₃** (Three I⁻ ions are needed to balance one Al³⁺ ion)
*   **Al³⁺ + Se²⁻ → Al₂Se₃** (Two Al³⁺ ions (+6 total) are needed to balance three Se²⁻ ions (-6 total))
*   **Al³⁺ + O²⁻ → Al₂O₃** (Two Al³⁺ ions (+6 total) are needed to balance three O²⁻ ions (-6 total))

### **Summary of Formulas**

Here are the nine predicted formulas in a table format:

|       | **I**   | **Se**    | **O**   |
| :---- | :------ | :-------- | :------ |
| **Na**  | NaI     | Na₂Se     | Na₂O    |
| **Sr**  | SrI₂    | SrSe      | SrO     |
| **Al**  | AlI₃    | Al₂Se₃    | Al₂O₃   |

Topic: 18.1 Periodicity
Question: Sodium chloride and strontium chloride are both white solids. How could you distinguish one from the other?
Answer: The possible ways of distinguishing between the two include infrared spectroscopy by comparison of known compounds, a flame test that gives the characteristic yellow color for sodium (strontium has a red flame), or comparison of their solubilities in water. At 20 °C, NaCl dissolves to the extent of $$\frac{\text{35.7 g}}{\text{100 mL}}$$ compared with $$\frac{\text{53.8 g}}{\text{100 mL}}$$ for SrCl_2. Heating to 100 °C provides an easy test, since the solubility of NaCl is $$\frac{\text{39.12 g}}{\text{100 mL}} ,$$ but that of SrCl_2 is $$\frac{\text{100.8 g}}{\text{100 mL}} .$$ Density determination on a solid is sometimes difficult, but there is enough difference (2.165 g/mL NaCl and 3.052 g/mL SrCl_2) that this method would be viable and perhaps the easiest and least expensive test to perform.
o3 Answer:
Dissolve a little of the solid in water and add a source of sulfate ions, e.g. a few drops of dilute H₂SO₄ (or aqueous Na₂SO₄).

• Strontium chloride: Sr²⁺ + SO₄²⁻ → SrSO₄(s)  
  A dense white precipitate of insoluble strontium sulfate forms.

• Sodium chloride: Na⁺ + SO₄²⁻ → (no precipitate)  
  The solution stays clear because all sodium salts are soluble.

Hence the sample that forms the white sulfate precipitate is strontium chloride; the one that remains clear is sodium chloride. (A flame test would also work: Na⁺ gives an intense yellow flame, Sr²⁺ a crimson-red flame.)
o4-mini Answer:
You can tell them apart very simply by either a flame‐test or a precipitation test:  

1.  Flame test  
   • Dissolve a little of each solid in water and dip a clean platinum (or nichrome) wire loop into each solution.  
   • Hold the loop in a Bunsen‐burner flame.  
     – Sodium gives a strong yellow (golden) flame.  
     – Strontium gives a crimson (red) flame.  

2.  Sulfate (or carbonate) precipitation  
   • Dissolve each solid in water.  
   • Add a few drops of dilute sulfuric acid or sodium sulfate.  
     – SrCl₂ → SrSO₄(s) gives a white precipitate.  
     – NaCl stays in solution (Na₂SO₄ is soluble).  

Either test cleanly distinguishes NaCl from SrCl₂.

   - **Sodium chloride (NaCl)**: When heated in a flame, sodium ions emit a **bright yellow** color.
   - **Strontium chloride (SrCl₂)**: Strontium ions produce a **crimson red** flame color.
   - This is the easiest and most distinctive way to differentiate them.

### 2. **Solubility Test**:
   - Both compounds are soluble in water, but their solubilities differ slightly:
     - NaCl is highly soluble (~35.9 g/100 mL at 25°C).
     - SrCl₂ is also soluble but less so (~53.8 g/100 mL at 25°C).

   - **Strontium chloride (SrCl₂)** reacts with sulfate ions (e.g., from sodium sulfate, Na₂SO₄) to form a **white precipitate of strontium sulfate (SrSO₄)**:
     \[
     SrCl₂ + Na₂SO₄ → SrSO₄ (s) + 2 NaCl

### 4. **Density Measurement**:
   - Strontium chloride (SrCl₂) has a higher density (~3.05 g/cm³) compared to sodium chloride (~2.16 g/cm³). Measuring the mass of a known volume can help distinguish them.

### **Best Method**:
The **flame test** is the most reliable and quickest way to distinguish between NaCl and SrCl₂ due to their distinct flame colors.

Would you like further clarification on any of these methods?
gemini-2.5-pro Answer:
Of course. Here is a detailed solution explaining how to distinguish between sodium chloride (NaCl) and strontium chloride (SrCl₂).

### Problem

Sodium chloride and strontium chloride are both white, crystalline solids that are soluble in water. How could you distinguish one from the other?

### Background Chemistry (The "Periodicity" Link)

This problem relates to periodicity because sodium (Na) and strontium (Sr) are in different groups of the periodic table, which gives them distinct chemical and physical properties.

*   **Sodium (Na)** is in Group 1 (the alkali metals). Its ions (Na⁺) have characteristic properties.
*   **Strontium (Sr)** is in Group 2 (the alkaline earth metals). Its ions (Sr²⁺) have different characteristic properties.

We can exploit these differences to identify them.

---

### Solution

There are two common and effective laboratory methods to distinguish between sodium chloride and strontium chloride.

### Method 1: The Flame Test (Primary Method)

This is the quickest and most definitive method, directly utilizing a key periodic property of these metals.

**Principle:** When metal ions are heated to a high temperature in a flame, their electrons get excited to higher energy levels. As they fall back to their normal energy levels, they emit light of a specific wavelength, which we perceive as a characteristic color. The color is unique to the metal ion.

**Procedure:**
1.  Take a clean wire loop (usually nichrome or platinum). To ensure it's clean, dip it in concentrated hydrochloric acid and heat it in the hottest part of a Bunsen burner flame until it no longer imparts any color to the flame.
2.  Dip the clean loop into a small sample of the first white solid, so a few crystals adhere to it.
3.  Introduce the loop into the edge of the Bunsen burner flame.
4.  Observe and record the color of the flame.
5.  Clean the loop thoroughly and repeat the test with the second white solid.

**Expected Results:**
*   **Sodium Chloride (NaCl):** Will produce a strong, persistent **intense yellow-orange** flame.
*   **Strontium Chloride (SrCl₂):** Will produce a vibrant **bright red** or **crimson** flame.

**Conclusion:** The distinct and different colors produced in the flame test allow for easy and unambiguous identification of the two compounds.

| Compound | Cation | Flame Color |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Sodium Chloride | Na⁺ | **Intense Yellow-Orange** |
| Strontium Chloride | Sr²⁺ | **Bright Red (Crimson)** |

---

### Method 2: Precipitation Reaction

This method uses the different solubility rules for Group 1 and Group 2 compounds.

**Principle:** All common sodium (Group 1) salts are soluble in water. However, some strontium (Group 2) salts, such as strontium sulfate, are insoluble. We can add a reagent that will form a precipitate with one ion but not the other.

**Procedure:**
1.  Take two separate test tubes. In each, dissolve a small amount (a spatula tip) of one of the unknown white solids in deionized water to create two clear, colorless solutions. Label them A and B.
2.  To each test tube, add a few drops of a soluble sulfate solution, such as **sodium sulfate (Na₂SO₄)** or **dilute sulfuric acid (H₂SO₄)**.
3.  Gently shake or swirl the test tubes and observe what happens.

**Expected Results:**
*   **Sodium Chloride (NaCl) solution:** **No change will be observed.** No precipitate will form because sodium sulfate is soluble in water. The solution will remain clear.
*   **Strontium Chloride (SrCl₂) solution:** A **white precipitate** will form. This precipitate is insoluble strontium sulfate (SrSO₄).
    *   The chemical equation for the reaction is:
        $SrCl₂(aq) + Na₂SO₄(aq) \rightarrow SrSO₄(s) + 2NaCl(aq)$

**Conclusion:** The formation of a white precipitate upon adding a sulfate solution identifies the compound as strontium chloride. The compound that results in no change is sodium chloride.

### Summary

| Test | Sodium Chloride (NaCl) | Strontium Chloride (SrCl₂) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Flame Test** | Intense Yellow-Orange Flame | Bright Red Flame |
| **Add Sulfate Solution** | No Reaction (remains clear) | Forms a White Precipitate |

gemini-2.5-pro Answer:
Of course. Here are the balanced equations for the reaction of elemental strontium (Sr) with each substance.

Strontium (Sr) is an alkaline earth metal in Group 2 of the periodic table. It is a reactive metal that typically loses two electrons to form the Sr²⁺ ion in its compounds.

---

### (a) Reaction with Oxygen

Strontium reacts with oxygen gas in a synthesis reaction to form the ionic compound strontium oxide.

*   **Reactants:** Strontium (Sr) and Oxygen (O₂)
*   **Products:** Strontium forms a +2 ion (Sr²⁺) and oxygen forms a -2 ion (O²⁻). They combine in a 1:1 ratio to form SrO.
*   **Unbalanced Equation:** Sr + O₂ → SrO
*   **Balanced Equation:**
    $2Sr(s) + O₂(g) \rightarrow 2SrO(s)$

---

### (b) Reaction with Hydrogen Bromide

As a reactive metal, strontium will displace hydrogen from an acid (like HBr) in a single displacement reaction. This produces a salt and hydrogen gas.

*   **Reactants:** Strontium (Sr) and Hydrogen Bromide (HBr)
*   **Products:** The salt is formed from the strontium ion (Sr²⁺) and the bromide ion (Br⁻), which gives the formula SrBr₂. The other product is hydrogen gas (H₂).
*   **Unbalanced Equation:** Sr + HBr → SrBr₂ + H₂
*   **Balanced Equation:**
    $Sr(s) + 2HBr(aq) \rightarrow SrBr₂(aq) + H₂(g)$

---

### (c) Reaction with Hydrogen

Strontium reacts with hydrogen gas, typically with heating, to form an ionic hydride. In metal hydrides, hydrogen takes on a -1 charge (the hydride ion, H⁻).

*   **Reactants:** Strontium (Sr) and Hydrogen (H₂)
*   **Products:** The strontium ion (Sr²⁺) combines with the hydride ion (H⁻) to form strontium hydride, SrH₂.
*   **Unbalanced Equation:** Sr + H₂ → SrH₂
*   **Balanced Equation:** (This equation is already balanced)
    $Sr(s) + H₂(g) \rightarrow SrH₂(s)$

---

### (d) Reaction with Phosphorus

Strontium reacts with phosphorus in a synthesis reaction to form the ionic compound strontium phosphide.

*   **Reactants:** Strontium (Sr) and Phosphorus (P)
*   **Products:** Strontium forms a +2 ion (Sr²⁺). Phosphorus, in Group 15, typically forms a -3 ion (the phosphide ion, P³⁻). To balance the charges, you need three Sr²⁺ ions (total charge +6) and two P³⁻ ions (total charge -6). The formula is Sr₃P₂.
*   **Unbalanced Equation:** Sr + P → Sr₃P₂
*   **Balanced Equation:**
    $3Sr(s) + 2P(s) \rightarrow Sr₃P₂(s)$

---

### (e) Reaction with Water

Like other active alkaline earth metals, strontium reacts with water to produce a metal hydroxide and hydrogen gas.

*   **Reactants:** Strontium (Sr) and Water (H₂O)
*   **Products:** The hydroxide is formed from the strontium ion (Sr²⁺) and the hydroxide ion (OH⁻), giving the formula Sr(OH)₂. The other product is hydrogen gas (H₂).
*   **Unbalanced Equation:** Sr + H₂O → Sr(OH)₂ + H₂
*   **Balanced Equation:**
    $Sr(s) + 2H₂O(l) \rightarrow Sr(OH)₂(aq) + H₂(g)$

Question: What is the mass of fish, in kilograms, that one would have to consume to obtain a fatal dose of mercury, if the fish contains 30 parts per million of mercury by weight? (Assume that all the mercury from the fish ends up as mercury(II) chloride in the body and that a fatal dose is 0.20 g of HgCl_2.) How many pounds of fish is this?
Answer: 11 lb
o3 Answer:
Step 1.  How much mercury is present in a lethal 0.20 g dose of HgCl₂ ?

Molar mass  
HgCl₂  = 200.59 (Hg) + 2·35.45 (Cl) ≈ 271.5 g mol⁻¹

Mass fraction of Hg in HgCl₂  
= 200.59 / 271.5 ≈ 0.739

Hg contained in 0.20 g of HgCl₂  
0.20 g × 0.739 ≈ 0.148 g Hg

Step 2.  Mercury concentration in the fish

30 ppm by weight = 30 mg Hg per 1 kg fish  
= 0.030 g Hg per 1 kg fish

Step 3.  Kilograms of fish that supply 0.148 g Hg

mass of fish = 0.148 g Hg ÷ 0.030 g Hg kg⁻¹  
              ≈ 4.9 kg

Step 4.  Convert kilograms to pounds

1 kg = 2.205 lb  
4.9 kg × 2.205 lb kg⁻¹ ≈ 11 lb

Answer  
About 5 kilograms (≈11 pounds) of such fish would have to be eaten to reach a fatal dose of mercury.

Topic: 18.1 Periodicity
Question: Does metallic tin react with HCl?
Answer: Yes, tin reacts with hydrochloric acid to produce hydrogen gas.
o3 Answer:
Yes. Tin is slightly more active than hydrogen (E° for Sn²⁺/Sn = –0.14 V), so the overall cell potential for

Sn(s) + 2 H⁺(aq)  →  Sn²⁺(aq) + H₂(g)

is E° = 0.00 V – (–0.14 V) = +0.14 V; the reaction is thermodynamically favorable. In hydrochloric acid the tin therefore dissolves, albeit rather slowly because the metal is covered with a thin, adherent SnO₂ film. Once that film is breached the reaction proceeds:

Sn(s) + 2 HCl(aq)  →  SnCl₂(aq) + H₂(g)↑
o4-mini Answer:
Yes – tin metal will dissolve in hydrochloric acid to give tin(II) chloride and hydrogen.  The overall reaction is

  Sn(s)  +  2 HCl(aq)  →  SnCl₂(aq)  +  H₂(g)

A few points to bear in mind:

1. Thermodynamics  
   The standard potentials are  
     Sn²⁺ + 2 e⁻ → Sn(s) E° = –0.136 V  
     2 H⁺ + 2 e⁻ → H₂(g) E° =  0.000 V  
   so E°cell = 0.000 – (–0.136) = +0.136 V, and the reaction is spontaneous.

2. Passivation  
   Fresh tin dissolves readily, but tin metal often carries a thin oxide (or hydroxide) layer that slows the attack of acid. Once that layer is eaten away, the reaction proceeds more quickly.

3. Oxidation state  
   Hydrochloric acid is only a moderately strong oxidizing agent (it supplies H⁺ but no stronger oxidant), so tin goes to the +2 state.  To get Sn(IV) (e.g. SnCl₄) you need a more powerful oxidizer (e.g. hot, concentrated nitric or sulfuric acid).

4. Periodicity context  
   In Group 14 the carbon–hydrogen‐acid chemistry is very different: carbon won’t react with HCl, silicon reacts by forming chlorosilanes, but tin behaves like a “typical” active metal in acid, liberating H₂.

So in practice: if you drop a clean piece of tin into dilute HCl, you will see bubbles of hydrogen and a clear solution of SnCl₂ building up.

gemini-2.5-pro Answer:
Of course. Here is a detailed solution explaining the reaction of metallic tin with hydrochloric acid, framed within the context of periodicity.

### The Short Answer

**Yes**, metallic tin (Sn) reacts with hydrochloric acid (HCl).

### The Chemical Reaction

The reaction is a single displacement (redox) reaction where tin is oxidized and the hydrogen ions from the acid are reduced. The primary products are aqueous tin(II) chloride and hydrogen gas.

The balanced chemical equation is:

**Sn(s) + 2 HCl(aq) → SnCl₂(aq) + H₂(g)**

*   **Observations:** You would see bubbles of hydrogen gas forming on the surface of the tin metal. The tin metal would slowly dissolve to form a colorless solution of tin(II) chloride. The reaction is typically slow at room temperature but can be accelerated by heating.

### The Explanation (Connecting to Periodicity and Chemistry Principles)

We can determine that this reaction will occur using two key principles related to chemical periodicity and reactivity:

#### 1. The Activity Series of Metals

The activity series is a list of metals ranked in order of decreasing reactivity. A metal can displace any metal (or hydrogen) below it in the series from a compound (or acid).

A simplified portion of the activity series looks like this:
...
Iron (Fe)
**Tin (Sn)**
Lead (Pb)
**Hydrogen (H)**
Copper (Cu)
Silver (Ag)
Gold (Au)
...

*   **Analysis:** Tin (Sn) is located **above** Hydrogen (H) in the activity series.
*   **Conclusion:** This indicates that tin is more reactive than hydrogen and is therefore capable of displacing hydrogen ions (H⁺) from an acid like HCl to form hydrogen gas (H₂).

#### 2. Standard Electrode Potentials (E°)

A more quantitative way to predict the spontaneity of a redox reaction is by calculating the standard cell potential (E°_cell). A reaction is spontaneous if its E°_cell is positive.

The overall reaction can be broken down into two half-reactions:

*   **Oxidation:** Tin metal loses electrons.
    `Sn(s) → Sn²⁺(aq) + 2e⁻`
    *The standard potential for this oxidation is E°_ox = +0.14 V*
    *(Note: The standard reduction potential for Sn²⁺ + 2e⁻ → Sn is -0.14 V. For oxidation, we reverse the sign.)*

*   **Reduction:** Hydrogen ions gain electrons.
    `2H⁺(aq) + 2e⁻ → H₂(g)`
    *The standard reduction potential for this is, by definition, E°_red = 0.00 V*

Now, we calculate the overall cell potential:

E°_cell = E°_oxidation + E°_redution
E°_cell = (+0.14 V) + (0.00 V)
**E°_cell = +0.14 V**

*   **Conclusion:** Since the overall cell potential is **positive**, the reaction is thermodynamically spontaneous and will proceed as written.

### Context within Group 14 (Periodicity)

Looking at tin's position in Group 14 helps us understand its metallic character and reactivity.

*   **Group 14:** Carbon (C), Silicon (Si), Germanium (Ge), **Tin (Sn)**, Lead (Pb)
*   **Trend:** As you move down Group 14, metallic character increases.
    *   **Carbon** is a non-metal and does not react with HCl.
    *   **Silicon** and **Germanium** are metalloids and are generally unreactive with non-oxidizing acids like HCl.
    *   **Tin** and **Lead** are metals that are reactive enough to react with HCl.

**An Important Note on Lead (Pb):**
Lead (Pb), the element below tin, also has a positive E°_cell for its reaction with HCl. However, the reaction `Pb(s) + 2HCl(aq) → PbCl₂(s) + H₂(g)` quickly stops. This is because lead(II) chloride (PbCl₂) is insoluble and forms a protective, non-reactive layer on the surface of the lead metal, preventing the acid from reaching the fresh metal underneath. This phenomenon is called **passivation**. Tin(II) chloride (SnCl₂), by contrast, is soluble, so the reaction can proceed until the tin is consumed.

Topic: 18.1 Periodicity
Question: Compare the nature of the bonds in PbCl_2 to that of the bonds in PbCl_4.
Answer: In PbCl_2, the bonding is ionic, as indicated by its melting point of 501 °C. In PbCl_4, the bonding is covalent, as evidenced by it being an unstable liquid at room temperature.
o3 Answer:
PbCl₂  
• Lead is only in the +2 oxidation state.  
• Pb²⁺ is quite large and has a relatively low charge density, so it does not polarise the electron cloud of Cl⁻ very strongly.  
• Consequently the bonding is predominantly ionic: a lattice of Pb²⁺ and Cl⁻ ions.  
• Consistent properties: high m.p., non-volatile, conducts when molten, insoluble in non-polar solvents.

PbCl₄  
• Lead is now in the +4 oxidation state.  
• Pb⁴⁺ is much smaller and carries twice the charge, so its polarising power is very high (Fajans’ rules).  
• It drags electron density off the chloride ions and the Pb–Cl links become shared-electron (covalent) bonds.  
• The compound exists as discrete tetrahedral PbCl₄ molecules; it is a volatile yellow liquid, does not conduct when molten and dissolves in covalent solvents.  

Thus: PbCl₂ is mainly ionic, whereas PbCl₄ is essentially covalent.
o4-mini Answer:
Here is a brief point-by-point comparison of PbCl₂ versus PbCl₄:

1. Oxidation state and “inert-pair” effect  
   – In PbCl₂ Pb is in the +II state, which is the more stable state for the heavy p-block metals because the 6s “inert pair” resists ionization.  
   – In PbCl₄ Pb is in the +IV state, forcing the 6s electrons into bonding and thus paying a large energetic penalty (hence PbCl₄ is relatively unstable and a strong oxidizer).

2. Crystal/molecular structure  
   – PbCl₂ is a high‐melting ionic solid.  Each Pb²⁺ is surrounded by several Cl⁻ in a distorted layered lattice; the bonding is largely electrostatic with some covalent polarization.  
   – PbCl₄ is a low‐melting, covalent molecular compound.  It exists as discrete tetrahedral PbCl₄ molecules (no extended ionic lattice).

3. Ionic vs. covalent character  
   – PbCl₂: Δχ(Pb–Cl) is large enough that the lattice is built up by Pb²⁺···Cl⁻ attractions.  Pb²⁺ is quite polarizing, so the bonds have partial covalent character, but the dominant force is ionic.  
   – PbCl₄: Pb⁴⁺ is extremely polarizing, so the Cl ligands share electron density covalently in directional Pb–Cl bonds.  There is effectively no ionic lattice.

4. Physical and chemical consequences  
   – PbCl₂ is stable to heating and only sparingly soluble in cold water (classic ionic‐solid behavior).  
   – PbCl₄ is thermally unstable (decomposes to PbCl₂ + Cl₂), hydrolyzes readily, and behaves as a covalent molecular species.  

Summary: Because of the inert‐pair effect, Pb(II) chloride adopts an ionic (but polarizable) lattice, whereas Pb(IV) chloride forms discrete covalent tetrahedral molecules.
deepseek-chat Answer:
To compare the nature of the bonds in **PbCl₂** and **PbCl₄**, we need to consider the **oxidation states of lead (Pb)**, **electronegativity differences**, and **bond type (ionic vs. covalent)**.

### **1. Oxidation State of Lead (Pb)**
- In **PbCl₂**, lead is in the **+2 oxidation state** (Pb²⁺).
- In **PbCl₄**, lead is in the **+4 oxidation state** (Pb⁴⁺).

### **2. Electronegativity & Bond Polarity**
- Chlorine (Cl) is highly electronegative (3.16), while lead (Pb) has an electronegativity of **1.87**.
- The **Pb–Cl bond** is **polar covalent** because of the significant electronegativity difference (ΔEN = 1.29).
- However, **Pb⁴⁺ (in PbCl₄)** is a smaller, more highly charged ion compared to **Pb²⁺ (in PbCl₂)**, leading to stronger polarization of the electron cloud by Pb⁴⁺.

### **3. Bond Nature**
- **PbCl₂** is **more ionic** because Pb²⁺ is less polarizing (due to lower charge) and forms bonds with more ionic character.
- **PbCl₄** is **more covalent** because Pb⁴⁺ has a higher charge density, polarizing the Cl⁻ electron clouds more effectively, increasing covalent character.

### **4. Stability & Inert Pair Effect**
- The **inert pair effect** makes the **+2 oxidation state (PbCl₂) more stable** than the **+4 state (PbCl₄)** for lead.
- PbCl₄ is **less stable** and can decompose into PbCl₂ and Cl₂.

### **Conclusion:**
| Property       | **PbCl₂** (Pb²⁺) | **PbCl₄** (Pb⁴⁺) |
|---------------|------------------|------------------|
| **Oxidation State** | +2 | +4 |
| **Bond Nature** | More ionic | More covalent |
| **Stability** | More stable (inert pair effect) | Less stable (can decompose) |

Thus, **PbCl₂ has more ionic bonds**, while **PbCl₄ has more covalent bonds** due to the higher polarizing power of Pb⁴⁺.
gemini-2.5-pro Answer:
Of course. Here is a detailed solution comparing the nature of the bonds in Lead(II) chloride (PbCl₂) and Lead(IV) chloride (PbCl₄).

### **Summary Answer**

The bonds in **PbCl₂ are predominantly ionic**, while the bonds in **PbCl₄ are predominantly covalent**. This difference is primarily explained by the oxidation state of the lead atom and the resulting polarizing power of the lead cation, as described by Fajan's Rules.

---

### **Detailed Explanation**

To understand the difference in bonding, we need to consider two key concepts: the **Inert Pair Effect** and **Fajan's Rules**.

#### 1. The Role of Lead's Oxidation State (The Inert Pair Effect)

*   **Lead (Pb)** is in Group 14 of the periodic table with the electron configuration [Xe] 4f¹⁴ 5d¹⁰ **6s² 6p²**.
*   It can form two common positive ions (cations) by losing electrons:
    *   **Pb²⁺:** By losing the two 6p electrons. The two 6s electrons remain.
    *   **Pb⁴⁺:** By losing all four valence electrons (the two 6p and the two 6s electrons).
*   For heavy elements at the bottom of the p-block like lead, the **inert pair effect** becomes significant. This effect describes the reluctance of the outermost s-electrons (the 6s² pair in lead) to be involved in bonding.
*   **Consequence:** The +2 oxidation state (forming Pb²⁺) is more stable and common for lead than the +4 oxidation state (forming Pb⁴⁺). This is why PbCl₂ is a stable solid, while PbCl₄ is an unstable liquid that readily decomposes.

#### 2. Applying Fajan's Rules to Compare Bond Character

Fajan's Rules predict the degree of covalent character in an ionic bond. Covalent character is favored when the cation can **polarize** (distort) the electron cloud of the anion.

**Greater covalent character results from:**
1.  **High positive charge** on the cation.
2.  **Small size** of the cation.
3.  **Large size** of the anion.

Let's apply these rules to compare PbCl₂ and PbCl₄:

| Factor | PbCl₂ (Lead(II) Chloride) | PbCl₄ (Lead(IV) Chloride) | Analysis |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Cation** | Pb²⁺ | Pb⁴⁺ | Pb⁴⁺ has a **higher positive charge**. |
| **Cation Size** | The Pb²⁺ ion is larger. | The Pb⁴⁺ ion is **much smaller** (it has lost two more electrons, so the nucleus pulls the remaining electrons in more tightly). | The smaller Pb⁴⁺ has a higher charge density. |
| **Anion** | Cl⁻ | Cl⁻ | The anion is the same in both compounds. |

**Conclusion from Fajan's Rules:**

The **Pb⁴⁺** cation in PbCl₄ has a much **higher charge** and **smaller size** than the Pb²⁺ cation in PbCl₂. This gives the Pb⁴⁺ ion a very high **charge density** and, therefore, a very strong **polarizing power**.

*   In **PbCl₄**, the powerful Pb⁴⁺ ion strongly attracts and distorts the electron clouds of the chloride ions, pulling them towards itself. This leads to a significant sharing of electron density between the lead and chlorine atoms, which is the definition of a **covalent bond**.
*   In **PbCl₂**, the Pb²⁺ ion has a lower charge density and is less able to polarize the chloride ions. Therefore, the bond remains largely electrostatic, resulting from the attraction between the distinct Pb²⁺ and Cl⁻ ions, which is characteristic of an **ionic bond**.

### **Comparison of Physical Properties**

The difference in bonding character is directly reflected in the physical properties of the two compounds, providing strong evidence for our conclusion.

| Property | PbCl₂ (More Ionic) | PbCl₄ (More Covalent) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Physical State** (at room temp.) | White crystalline solid | Yellow, fuming liquid |
| **Melting Point** | High: 501 °C | Very Low: -15 °C |
| **Structure** | Ionic crystal lattice | Discrete tetrahedral molecules (CCl₄ is a similar example) |
| **Stability** | Thermally stable | Thermally unstable; decomposes to PbCl₂ and Cl₂ gas (PbCl₄ → PbCl₂ + Cl₂) |

### **Final Summary**

*   **PbCl₂:** The +2 oxidation state of lead results in a relatively large cation (Pb²⁺) with a low charge density. This leads to weak polarization of the chloride ion, and the compound exhibits the properties of a typical **ionic solid** with high melting point.
*   **PbCl₄:** The +4 oxidation state of lead results in a small, highly charged cation (Pb⁴⁺) with a high charge density. This strongly polarizes the chloride ions, leading to significant electron sharing. The compound behaves as a **molecular covalent liquid** with a very low melting point.
gpt-5 Answer:
- PbCl2: Predominantly ionic. It forms a crystalline lattice of Pb2+ and Cl−; bonding has some covalent character but is mostly ionic.
- PbCl4: Predominantly covalent. It exists as discrete tetrahedral molecules (PbCl4), with strong polarization by Pb4+ leading to covalent Pb–Cl bonds; it is much less stable and readily decomposes to PbCl2 + Cl2.

Reason: By Fajans’ rules, the highly charged, smaller Pb4+ cation polarizes Cl− strongly, favoring covalency, whereas Pb2+ polarizes less, favoring ionic character.

\[

     \]

This reaction is feasible because calcium is more electropositive (more willing to lose electrons) than cesium, allowing it to displace cesium from its compound.

Question: Give balanced equations for the overall reaction in the electrolysis of molten lithium chloride and for the reactions occurring at the electrodes. You may wish to review the chapter on electrochemistry for relevant examples.
Answer: Cathode (reduction): $$\text{2Li}^{+} + \text{2e}^{-} ⟶ \text{2Li} ( l ) \text{;}$$ Anode (oxidation): $$\text{2Cl}^{-} ⟶ \text{Cl}_{2} ( g ) + \text{2e}^{\text{unicode[:-]}} ;$$ Overall reaction: $$\text{2Li}^{+} + \text{2Cl}^{-} ⟶ \text{2Li} ( l ) + \text{Cl}_{2} ( g )$$
o3 Answer:
Electrolysis is carried out on molten lithium chloride, LiCl(l), so the only species present are Li⁺(l) and Cl⁻(l).

Cathode (reduction)
Li⁺(l) + e⁻  ⟶  Li(l)                        (1)

Anode (oxidation)
2 Cl⁻(l) ⟶ Cl₂(g) + 2 e⁻                (2)

To balance the electrons, multiply equation (1) by 2 and add to equation (2):

2 Li⁺(l) + 2 e⁻ ⟶ 2 Li(l)  
2 Cl⁻(l) ⟶ Cl₂(g) + 2 e⁻  
——————————————  
2 Li⁺(l) + 2 Cl⁻(l) ⟶ 2 Li(l) + Cl₂(g)

Since Li⁺ + Cl⁻ together constitute LiCl, the overall cell reaction is

2 LiCl(l) ⟶ 2 Li(l) + Cl₂(g)

(or equivalently LiCl(l) ⟶ Li(l) + ½ Cl₂(g)).

gemini-2.5-pro Answer:
Of course. Let's break down the electrolysis of molten lithium chloride (LiCl).

Electrolysis is the process of using a direct electric current (DC) to drive an otherwise non-spontaneous chemical reaction. For the electrolysis of a molten ionic salt like LiCl, the process is as follows:

1.  **Dissociation:** In its molten (liquid) state, lithium chloride dissociates into its constituent ions:
    $LiCl(l) → Li⁺(l) + Cl⁻(l)$

2.  **Migration of Ions:** An electric current is passed through the molten salt via two electrodes:
    *   The **cathode** is the negative electrode. It attracts the positive ions (cations), which are $Li⁺$.
    *   The **anode** is the positive electrode. It attracts the negative ions (anions), which are $Cl⁻$.

### **Reactions at the Electrodes**

Here are the balanced equations for the half-reactions that occur at each electrode.

#### **1. Reaction at the Cathode (Reduction)**

At the cathode, the lithium cations ($Li⁺$) gain electrons and are reduced to form liquid lithium metal. (Note: The melting point of LiCl is 610 °C, and the melting point of Li is 180.5 °C, so the lithium metal produced will be in a liquid state).

*   **Half-Reaction at the Cathode:**
    $Li⁺(l) + e⁻ → Li(l)$

#### **2. Reaction at the Anode (Oxidation)**

At the anode, the chloride anions ($Cl⁻$) lose electrons and are oxidized to form chlorine gas ($Cl₂$). Since chlorine gas is diatomic, two chloride ions must react to form one molecule of $Cl₂$.

*   **Half-Reaction at the Anode:**
    $2Cl⁻(l) → Cl₂(g) + 2e⁻$

### **Overall Reaction**

To get the overall balanced equation, we combine the two half-reactions. The number of electrons lost in oxidation must equal the number of electrons gained in reduction.

1.  **Cathode:** $Li⁺(l) + e⁻ → Li(l)$
2.  **Anode:** $2Cl⁻(l) → Cl₂(g) + 2e⁻$

To balance the electrons, we must multiply the entire cathode reaction by 2:

*   **Balanced Cathode Reaction:** $2Li⁺(l) + 2e⁻ → 2Li(l)$

Now, we can add the balanced cathode and anode reactions together:

$(2Li⁺(l) + 2e⁻) + (2Cl⁻(l)) → (2Li(l)) + (Cl₂(g) + 2e⁻)$

Finally, we cancel the electrons ($2e⁻$) from both sides of the equation to get the final overall reaction. We can also combine the reactant ions ($2Li⁺$ and $2Cl⁻$) back into the parent compound, $2LiCl$.

*   **Overall Balanced Equation:**
    **$2LiCl(l) → 2Li(l) + Cl₂(g)$**

   n(Ca) = 10.01 g ÷ 40.078 g mol⁻¹ = 0.2497 mol Ca

   n(H₂) = 0.2497 mol

3. Convert moles of H₂ to grams  
   M(H₂) = 2.01588 g mol⁻¹

Topic: 18.2 Occurrence and Preparation of the Representative Metals
Question: Magnesium is an active metal; it burns in the form of powder, ribbons, and filaments to provide flashes of brilliant light. Why is it possible to use magnesium in construction?
Answer: Despite its reactivity, magnesium can be used in construction even when the magnesium is going to come in contact with a flame because a protective oxide coating is formed, preventing gross oxidation. Only if the metal is finely subdivided or present in a thin sheet will a high-intensity flame cause its rapid burning.
o3 Answer:
Finely-divided magnesium (powder, ribbon, thin shavings) burns readily because almost every atom is on the surface, the heat that is produced cannot be carried away fast enough, and the thin oxide skin that forms is continuously ruptured.  

With bulk magnesium (bars, plates, castings, structural alloys) two things prevent combustion:

1. A very thin, tenacious layer of magnesium oxide (and hydroxide) forms instantly in air.  
   • It adheres tightly to the metal.  
   • It is refractory and non-porous, so it stops oxygen from reaching the underlying metal.

2. The surface-area-to-volume ratio is small. The heat generated at the surface is rapidly conducted away into the interior of the metal; the temperature never rises to the ignition point.

Because of these two effects, massive pieces or alloys of magnesium are not easily ignited and resist further corrosion, making the metal usable in structural applications despite its high chemical reactivity when finely divided.
o4-mini Answer:
Magnesium’s violent behavior is an artifact of having a very large surface‐to‐volume ratio (powder, ribbon, filament) so that it ignites easily.  In bulk form, however, two things tame its reactivity:

1. Formation of a thin, adherent oxide (MgO) “skin.”  As soon as a piece of magnesium is exposed to air it is coated with a passive layer of MgO.  That film blocks further attack by oxygen or water.  
2. Low surface area.  A solid beam or plate of magnesium has comparatively little surface exposed, so any slow corrosion or oxidation at that outer film affects only the surface.  The interior remains metallic and strong.

Together, the protective oxide layer and the low surface‐to‐volume ratio of a structural member make bulk magnesium both corrosion‐resistant enough and mechanically robust enough for use in construction and in lightweight alloys.
deepseek-chat Answer:
Magnesium can be used in construction despite being an active metal because of several key factors that mitigate its reactivity and enhance its practicality:

### 1. **Passivation (Protective Oxide Layer)**
   - When exposed to air, magnesium forms a thin, adherent layer of **magnesium oxide (MgO)** on its surface. This layer acts as a barrier, protecting the bulk metal from further oxidation or rapid corrosion in typical atmospheric conditions.

### 2. **Alloying for Stability**
   - Pure magnesium is rarely used in construction. Instead, it is alloyed with other metals (e.g., aluminum, zinc, or manganese) to improve:
     - **Corrosion resistance** (e.g., AZ91 alloy: 9% Al, 1% Zn).
     - **Mechanical strength** and durability.
     - **Workability** (e.g., extrusion or casting into structural components).

### 3. **Lightweight Advantage**
   - Magnesium alloys are **lighter than aluminum** (density ~1.74 g/cm³) and steel, making them ideal for applications where weight reduction is critical (e.g., aerospace, automotive, or portable structures).

### 4. **Controlled Environments**
   - In construction, magnesium alloys are typically used in **dry or low-moisture environments** to minimize corrosion risks. Protective coatings (paint, anodization) are also applied for added durability.

### 5. **Fire Safety Considerations**
   - While powdered or thin magnesium ribbons burn vigorously, **bulk magnesium** (e.g., thick structural components) is harder to ignite due to its high thermal conductivity, which dissipates heat. Fire-resistant treatments further reduce flammability risks.

### Common Construction Uses:
   - **Lightweight frames** (e.g., temporary structures).
   - **Cladding panels** (when alloyed and coated).
   - **Aerospace/transport infrastructure** (e.g., aircraft components, high-performance vehicles).

### Key Limitation:
   - Magnesium is avoided in **high-moisture or salt-rich environments** (e.g., marine applications) unless heavily protected, as chloride ions can break down the oxide layer.

In summary, magnesium’s usability in construction stems from **alloying, passivation, and smart engineering**—transforming a reactive metal into a functional, lightweight material.
gemini-2.5-pro Answer:
Of course. Here is the solution to the problem.

This question presents a classic paradox: If magnesium is highly flammable, how can it be a safe and viable material for construction?

The answer lies in the difference between the metal's properties in different forms and conditions. While magnesium powder or thin ribbons are indeed highly flammable, solid, bulk magnesium is not.

Here are the key reasons why it is possible to use magnesium in construction:

### 1. The Importance of Form (Surface-Area-to-Volume Ratio)

The statement that magnesium "burns in the form of powder, ribbons, and filaments" is the most critical clue.

*   **High Surface Area:** Powders, ribbons, and shavings have an extremely high surface-area-to-volume ratio. This allows a large amount of the metal to be exposed to oxygen at once. It also means that heat cannot dissipate quickly, allowing the material to easily reach its ignition temperature (around 473 °C or 883 °F).
*   **Low Surface Area (Bulk Form):** A solid block, beam, or sheet of magnesium has a very low surface-area-to-volume ratio. It is a good thermal conductor, so it acts as its own **heat sink**. To ignite a solid piece of magnesium, you would need to apply a significant and sustained amount of heat to raise the entire bulk piece to its ignition temperature, which is very difficult to do in normal conditions.

Think of it like wood: sawdust can be explosive and kindling burns easily, but it is very difficult to set a large, solid log on fire with just a match.

### 2. The Protective Oxide Layer (Passivation)

Like its neighbor on the periodic table, aluminum, magnesium is an active metal that reacts quickly with oxygen in the air. This reaction forms a thin, but very durable and stable, layer of **magnesium oxide (MgO)** on the surface.

$$2 \text{Mg(s)} + \text{O}_2\text{(g)} \rightarrow 2 \text{MgO(s)}$$

This dull gray oxide layer is non-reactive and acts as a protective barrier, or **passivation layer**. It seals the highly reactive pure magnesium underneath from further contact with oxygen, preventing further oxidation and corrosion under normal atmospheric conditions.

### 3. Use in Alloys, Not Pure Form

In virtually all structural applications, pure magnesium is not used. Instead, it is combined with other metals to form **alloys**. The most common alloying elements are aluminum, zinc, and manganese.

These alloys are engineered to:

*   **Increase Strength:** Pure magnesium is relatively soft. Alloying makes it much stronger and more durable.
*   **Improve Corrosion Resistance:** The alloys are designed to be more resistant to corrosion than pure magnesium.
*   **Enhance Stability:** Alloying can raise the ignition temperature and further reduce the material's flammability.

### Summary

It is possible to use magnesium in construction because the fire hazard associated with it exists almost exclusively when the metal is in a finely divided state (powder or shavings).

In its solid, bulk form used for construction and manufacturing, magnesium is safe for three main reasons:
1.  **Bulk Form:** It has a low surface-area-to-volume ratio and acts as its own heat sink, making it very difficult to ignite.
2.  **Oxide Layer:** It naturally forms a protective, non-reactive layer of magnesium oxide (MgO) that prevents it from reacting further with the environment.
3.  **Alloys:** It is used as an alloy, not a pure metal, which significantly improves its strength, stability, and resistance to corrosion.

These properties allow engineers to take advantage of magnesium's primary benefit: its incredible **strength-to-weight ratio**, making it ideal for applications in the aerospace, automotive, and electronics industries where light weight and high strength are critical.

Topic: 18.2 Occurrence and Preparation of the Representative Metals
Question: Describe the production of metallic aluminum by electrolytic reduction.
Answer: Extract from ore: $$\text{AlO} ( \text{OH} ) ( s ) + \text{NaOH} ( a q ) + \text{H}_{2} \text{O} ( l ) ⟶ \text{Na} [ \text{Al} ( \text{OH} )_{4} ] ( a q )$$ Recover: $$\text{2Na} [ \text{Al} ( \text{OH} )_{4} ] ( s ) + \text{H}_{2} \text{SO}_{4} ( a q ) ⟶ \text{2Al} ( \text{OH} )_{3} ( s ) + \text{Na}_{2} \text{SO}_{4} ( a q ) + \text{2H}_{2} \text{O} ( l )$$ Sinter: $$\text{2Al} ( \text{OH} )_{3} ( s ) ⟶ \text{Al}_{2} \text{O}_{3} ( s ) + \text{3H}_{2} \text{O} ( g )$$ Dissolve in Na_3AlF_6( l ) and electrolyze: $$\text{Al}^{3+} + \text{3e}^{-} ⟶ \text{Al} ( s )$$
o3 Answer:
Production of aluminum metal is carried out almost exclusively by the Hall–Héroult process, an electrolytic reduction of alumina (Al₂O₃).  The key ideas are:

1.  First convert the ore bauxite to pure alumina (Bayer process).  
2.  Dissolve the alumina in a molten salt (cryolite) so that it will conduct electricity at a temperature below its own 2050 °C melting point.  
3.  Pass a large direct current through the molten mixture; Al³⁺ ions are reduced at the cathode to liquid aluminum and O²⁻ ions are oxidized at the anode, where they react with the carbon anode to give CO and CO₂.  

DETAILS

1. Preparation of Al₂O₃ (Bayer process)  
   • Bauxite (mainly Al₂O₃·xH₂O with Fe₂O₃, SiO₂) is digested in hot concentrated NaOH.  
        Al₂O₃·xH₂O + 2 OH⁻ → 2 [Al(OH)₄]⁻  
   • Insoluble Fe, Ti oxides are filtered off (red mud).  
   • The solution is cooled, seeding precipitates Al(OH)₃.  
        [Al(OH)₄]⁻ + H⁺ → Al(OH)₃↓ + H₂O  
   • Al(OH)₃ is calcined (≈1100 °C) giving anhydrous alumina.  
        2 Al(OH)₃ → Al₂O₃ + 3 H₂O  

2. Electrolytic reduction (Hall–Héroult cell)  
   Cell construction  
   • Steel shell lined with carbon serves as the cathode.  
   • Pre-baked carbon blocks (or continuously fed carbon paste) are the consumable anodes suspended from the top.  
   • The electrolyte is molten cryolite, Na₃AlF₆, with 2–8 wt % CaF₂ and 3–5 wt % AlF₃ added to adjust melting point and conductivity.  
   • Operating temperature: 940–980 °C (well below the melting point of pure alumina).  
   • Alumina (≈4–5 wt %) is continually fed to replace that which is reduced.

   Principal electrode reactions  
   Cathode (carbon lining on the bottom):  
        Al³⁺ + 3 e⁻ → Al(l)            (reduction)  

   Anode (carbon blocks):  
        2 O²⁻ → O₂(g) + 4 e⁻          (oxidation)  
   The nascent oxygen immediately attacks the carbon anode:  
        C + O₂ → CO₂(g)         or   2 C + O₂ → 2 CO(g)  

   Net cell reaction (idealized):  
        2 Al₂O₃ + 3 C → 4 Al(l) + 3 CO₂(g)

3. Collection of aluminum  
   • Molten aluminum (density 2.3 g cm⁻³) collects at the bottom, beneath the less dense electrolyte.  
   • Metal is siphoned out periodically without shutting off the current.  
   • The consumed carbon anodes must be replaced regularly.

4. Energy and materials balance  
   • Typical cells operate at 4–5 V and 100–300 kA; energy demand ≈13–15 kWh per kg Al.  
   • About 0.4–0.5 kg of carbon anode is consumed per kg of aluminum produced.  

In summary, metallic aluminum is obtained by dissolving purified alumina in molten cryolite, then passing a large electric current through the melt.  Aluminum ions are reduced to liquid aluminum at the cathode, while oxide ions oxidize the carbon anodes, producing CO and CO₂; the overall effect is the electrolytic reduction of Al₂O₃ to Al metal.
o4-mini Answer:
Aluminum metal is produced industrially by electrolytic reduction of alumina (Al₂O₃) in what’s known as the Hall–Héroult process.  The overall scheme has two main stages: first refining bauxite to alumina (the Bayer process), then dissolving alumina in molten cryolite and electrolyzing it to metal.

1. From Bauxite to Alumina (Bayer Process)  
  • Bauxite (mainly Al(OH)₃ plus Fe and Si impurities) is digested under pressure in concentrated NaOH.  
  • Al(OH)₃ dissolves as NaAlO₂, impurities remain insoluble and are removed (“red mud”).  
  • Upon cooling and seeding, Al(OH)₃ precipitates.  
  • The hydroxide is filtered, washed, then calcined at ~1 100 °C to give pure Al₂O₃.

2. Electrolytic Reduction (Hall–Héroult Cell)  
  Cell Components & Conditions  
    – Electrolyte: a molten mixture of Al₂O₃ dissolved in cryolite (Na₃AlF₆), often with CaF₂ added, at ~950–980 °C. Cryolite lowers the melting point (~1 020 °C for pure alumina) and improves conductivity.  
    – Cathode: the steel or carbon-lined cell bottom. Molten aluminum collects here.  
    – Anodes: consumable prebaked carbon blocks immersed in the electrolyte.  

  Electrochemical Reactions  
    – At the cathode:  
        Al³⁺   + 3 e⁻ → Al(l)  
      Molten aluminum droplets sink to the bottom and are periodically tapped off.  
    – At the anode:  
        2 O²⁻ + C → CO₂ + 4 e⁻  
      (Some CO can form depending on conditions; net oxide removal is as CO₂.)  
    – Net cell reaction:  
        2 Al₂O₃ + 3 C → 4 Al + 3 CO₂  

  Cell Operation  
    • Voltage: typically 4–5 V per cell.  
    • Current density: on the order of 0.5–1 A/cm², total currents of 100–300 kA in large cells.  
    • Energy consumption: ~13–15 kWh of electric energy per kilogram of Al produced.  
    • Alumina feed: finely ground Al₂O₃ is regularly added to maintain concentration around 2–4 wt%.  
    • Anode replacement: carbon anodes are gradually consumed and must be changed every few weeks.  
    • Tap temperature: molten Al is withdrawn at ~950 °C, then cast or transferred to holding furnaces.

3. Environmental and Practical Considerations  
  • CO₂ emissions are inherent from anode oxidation. Efforts toward inert anodes (e.g., metal oxides) aim to reduce this.  
  • Fluorides (from cryolite) and particulates must be controlled to minimize atmospheric and water pollution.  
  • Efficient heat recovery and pre-baked anode technologies improve energy economy and reduce emissions.

In sum, metallic aluminum is obtained by electrolyzing alumina dissolved in a fluoride-based molten bath, producing Al at the cathode and oxidizing carbon anodes to CO₂. This Hall–Héroult electrolytic process, combined with the Bayer refining of bauxite, is the cornerstone of today’s aluminum industry.

### **1. Raw Material: Bauxite Ore**  
Aluminum is extracted from **bauxite** (Al₂O₃·xH₂O), which is purified to obtain **alumina (Al₂O₃)** via the **Bayer process**:

- **Electrolyte**: A molten mixture of **alumina (Al₂O₃)** dissolved in **cryolite (Na₃AlF₆)** (lowers melting point from ~2072°C to ~950°C).  
- **Anode**: Made of carbon (graphite).  
- **Cathode**: Lined with carbon at the bottom of the cell.  
- **Temperature**: Maintained at ~950°C.  

### **3. Electrolysis Reactions**  
- **Dissolution**: Al₂O₃ dissolves in cryolite, forming **Al³⁺** and **O²⁻** ions.  
- **Reduction (Cathode)**:  
  \[ \text{Al}^{3+} + 3e^- \rightarrow \text{Al (l)} } \]  
  Molten aluminum sinks to the bottom and is tapped off.  
- **Oxidation (Anode)**:  
  \[ 2\text{O}^{2-} + \text{C} \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + 4e^- \]

- **High Energy Consumption**: The process requires significant electricity (~15 kWh per kg of Al).  
- **Anode Replacement**: Carbon anodes are regularly replaced due to oxidation.  
- **Purity**: The aluminum produced is ~99.5% pure and may undergo further refining.

The molten aluminum is cast into **ingots** or **billets** for industrial use.  

### **Summary Equation**:  
\[ \text{Al}_2\text{O}_3 + 3\text{C} \rightarrow 2\text{Al} + 3\text{CO}_2 \]  

This process is the dominant industrial method due to its efficiency, despite its high energy demands.
gemini-2.5-pro Answer:
Of course. Here is a detailed description of the production of metallic aluminum by electrolytic reduction, a process known as the **Hall-Héroult process**.

The production of aluminum is a two-stage process. First, the primary ore must be purified, and second, the purified ore is electrolytically reduced to the pure metal.

### **Step 1: The Bayer Process (Purification of Bauxite Ore)**

The primary ore of aluminum is **bauxite**, a clay-like rock that is a mixture of hydrated aluminum oxide ($Al_2O_3 \cdot nH_2O$), iron(III) oxide ($Fe_2O_3$), silicon dioxide ($SiO_2$), and other impurities. The Hall-Héroult process requires very pure aluminum oxide, so the bauxite must be refined first.

1.  **Digestion:** The crushed bauxite ore is treated with a hot, concentrated solution of sodium hydroxide ($NaOH$) under pressure. The aluminum oxide is **amphoteric**, meaning it reacts with both acids and strong bases. It dissolves to form soluble sodium aluminate ($Na[Al(OH)_4]$).
    $Al_2O_3(s) + 2NaOH(aq) + 3H_2O(l) \rightarrow 2Na[Al(OH)_4](aq)$

2.  **Filtration:** The major impurities, such as iron(III) oxide and silicates, do not react with the strong base and remain solid. They are separated from the sodium aluminate solution by filtration. This solid waste is known as "red mud."

3.  **Precipitation:** The hot sodium aluminate solution is cooled. As it cools, its solubility decreases. The solution is "seeded" with crystals of pure aluminum hydroxide ($Al(OH)_3$) to induce the precipitation of solid aluminum hydroxide.
    $Na[Al(OH)_4](aq) \rightarrow Al(OH)_3(s) + NaOH(aq)$

4.  **Calcination:** The filtered aluminum hydroxide crystals are washed and then heated to a high temperature (around 1000-1200 °C) in a process called calcination. This drives off the water, leaving behind a fine white powder of pure aluminum oxide, commonly known as **alumina** ($Al_2O_3$).
    $2Al(OH)_3(s) \xrightarrow{\Delta} Al_2O_3(s) + 3H_2O(g)$

---

### **Step 2: The Hall-Héroult Process (Electrolytic Reduction of Alumina)**

This is the core of aluminum production where the pure alumina is reduced to metallic aluminum.

**The Challenge:** Alumina ($Al_2O_3$) has an extremely high melting point of over 2000 °C. Melting it for electrolysis would be prohibitively expensive and technically difficult.

**The Solution:** In 1886, Charles Hall (in the US) and Paul Héroult (in France) independently discovered that alumina dissolves in molten **cryolite** ($Na_3AlF_6$). This solution has a much lower melting point of about 950-1000 °C, making electrolysis economically feasible.

#### **The Electrolytic Cell:**

The process is carried out in a large rectangular steel vessel.
*   **Cathode (Negative Electrode):** The steel cell is lined with thick blocks of graphite (a form of carbon). This carbon lining serves as the cathode.
*   **Anode (Positive Electrode):** Large blocks of graphite are suspended from above and dip into the molten electrolyte. These serve as the anode.
*   **Electrolyte:** The cell is filled with molten cryolite, to which the purified alumina ($Al_2O_3$) is added and dissolved. Additives like calcium fluoride ($CaF_2$) are also used to further lower the melting point and improve conductivity.



#### **The Electrolysis Reactions:**

A high-amperage direct current is passed through the cell.

1.  **At the Cathode (Reduction):**
    The aluminum ions ($Al^{3+}$) from the dissolved alumina are attracted to the negative carbon lining (cathode). Here, they gain three electrons each and are reduced to molten aluminum metal.
    **Reaction:** $Al^{3+} + 3e^- \rightarrow Al(l)$

    The molten aluminum is denser than the cryolite electrolyte, so it sinks and collects at the bottom of the cell. It is periodically siphoned or tapped off.

2.  **At the Anode (Oxidation):**
    The oxide ions ($O^{2-}$) are attracted to the positive carbon anodes. Here, they lose two electrons each and are oxidized to form oxygen gas ($O_2$).
    **Reaction:** $2O^{2-} \rightarrow O_2(g) + 4e^-$

3.  **Anode Consumption (Side Reaction):**
    The temperature of the cell is very high (~1000 °C). The oxygen gas produced at the anodes immediately and aggressively reacts with the hot carbon anodes, forming carbon dioxide ($CO_2$) and some carbon monoxide ($CO$).
    **Reaction:** $C(s) + O_2(g) \rightarrow CO_2(g)$

    Because of this side reaction, the carbon anodes are rapidly consumed and must be replaced every few weeks. This is a significant operational cost of the process.

#### **Overall Reaction:**

If we combine the primary reduction/oxidation steps and the anode consumption, the overall chemical transformation in the cell is:

**$2Al_2O_3(dissolved) + 3C(s) \rightarrow 4Al(l) + 3CO_2(g)$**

### **Summary of Key Points:**

*   **Raw Material:** Bauxite ore is purified into alumina ($Al_2O_3$) via the **Bayer Process**.
*   **Solvent:** Alumina is dissolved in molten **cryolite** ($Na_3AlF_6$) to lower the operating temperature from >2000°C to ~1000°C.
*   **Electrodes:** The cell has a **carbon-lined cathode** and consumable **carbon anodes**.
*   **Reduction:** At the cathode, $Al^{3+}$ ions are reduced to form molten aluminum ($Al(l)$).
*   **Oxidation:** At the anode, $O^{2-}$ ions are oxidized to $O_2$ gas, which then reacts with the carbon anode to form $CO_2$.
*   **Energy:** The process is extremely **energy-intensive**, requiring vast amounts of electricity. This is why aluminum smelters are often located near sources of cheap and abundant power, such as hydroelectric dams.

Question: A chemist dissolves a 1.497-g sample of a type of metal (an alloy of Sn, Pb, Sb, and Cu) in nitric acid, and metastannic acid, H_2SnO_3, is precipitated. She heats the precipitate to drive off the water, which leaves 0.4909 g of tin(IV) oxide. What was the percentage of tin in the original sample?
o3 Answer:
The weighed product is SnO₂.  
From it we can calculate how much Sn was present, then relate that to the mass of the original alloy sample.

1. Molar mass of SnO₂  
   Sn = 118.71 g mol⁻¹  
   O₂ = 2 × 16.00 = 32.00 g mol⁻¹  
   M(SnO₂) = 118.71 + 32.00 = 150.71 g mol⁻¹

2. Mass fraction of Sn in SnO₂  
   mass fraction(Sn) = 118.71 / 150.71 = 0.7873

3. Mass of Sn obtained  
   m(Sn) = 0.7873 × 0.4909 g = 0.386 g  (0.3865 g with full digits)

4. Percentage of Sn in the alloy sample  
   %Sn = (mass Sn / mass sample) × 100  
   %Sn = (0.3865 g / 1.497 g) × 100 = 25.8 %

Tin constituted approximately 25.8 % of the original alloy.

We are told the final SnO₂ mass is 0.4909 g.  From this we find the moles of SnO₂, which equals the moles of Sn:

M(SnO₂) = 118.71 (Sn) + 2·16.00 (O) = 150.71 g·mol⁻¹  
moles SnO₂ = 0.4909 g ÷ 150.71 g·mol⁻¹ = 3.255·10⁻³ mol  
⇒ moles Sn = 3.255·10⁻³ mol

Finally, % Sn in the original 1.497 g sample =  
(0.3865 g ÷ 1.497 g)·100% = 25.8%  

Answer: The alloy was 25.8% Sn by mass.

Of course! Let's solve this step-by-step. This is a classic gravimetric analysis problem where we use the mass of a final product to determine the amount of a substance in the original sample.

### Principle

The key principle here is the **conservation of mass**. All the tin (Sn) present in the original alloy sample is converted into tin(IV) oxide ($SnO_2$). Therefore, if we can calculate the mass of tin in the final 0.4909 g of $SnO_2$, we will know the mass of tin that was in the original 1.497-g sample.

### Step 1: Find the Molar Masses

First, we need the molar masses of tin (Sn) and oxygen (O) to calculate the molar mass of tin(IV) oxide ($SnO_2$).

*   Molar Mass of Tin (Sn) = 118.71 g/mol
*   Molar Mass of Oxygen (O) = 16.00 g/mol

Now, calculate the molar mass of tin(IV) oxide ($SnO_2$):
Molar Mass of $SnO_2$ = (1 × Molar Mass of Sn) + (2 × Molar Mass of O)
Molar Mass of $SnO_2$ = (1 × 118.71 g/mol) + (2 × 16.00 g/mol)
Molar Mass of $SnO_2$ = 118.71 + 32.00
**Molar Mass of $SnO_2$ = 150.71 g/mol**

### Step 2: Calculate the Mass of Tin in the Tin(IV) Oxide

Next, we determine what fraction of the mass of $SnO_2$ is due to tin. We can use this fraction as a conversion factor to find the mass of tin in our 0.4909 g sample of $SnO_2$.

Mass Fraction of Sn in $SnO_2$ = $\frac{\text{Molar Mass of Sn}}{\text{Molar Mass of } SnO_2}$
Mass Fraction of Sn in $SnO_2$ = $\frac{118.71 \text{ g/mol}}{150.71 \text{ g/mol}}$

Now, use this fraction to find the mass of tin:
Mass of Sn = Mass of $SnO_2$ × (Mass Fraction of Sn in $SnO_2$)
Mass of Sn = 0.4909 g × ($\frac{118.71}{150.71}$)
Mass of Sn = 0.4909 g × 0.787678
**Mass of Sn = 0.38668 g**

### Step 3: Calculate the Percentage of Tin in the Original Sample

Finally, we can calculate the percentage of tin in the original alloy sample using the mass of tin we just found and the total mass of the original sample.

Percentage of Sn = ($\frac{\text{Mass of Sn}}{\text{Total Mass of Sample}}$) × 100%
Percentage of Sn = ($\frac{0.38668 \text{ g}}{1.497 \text{ g}}$) × 100%
Percentage of Sn = 0.25830 × 100%
**Percentage of Sn = 25.83%**

(The given values have four significant figures, so the answer is rounded to four significant figures).

***

### Final Answer

The percentage of tin in the original sample was **25.83%**.

This is an electrochemistry problem that uses Faraday's laws of electrolysis to relate the amount of electric charge passed through a solution to the amount of substance produced.

### Step 1: Identify the Half-Reaction

The problem involves the electrolysis of a magnesium chloride ($MgCl_2$) melt. To form solid magnesium metal, magnesium ions ($Mg^{2+}$) must be reduced at the cathode. Reduction is the gain of electrons.

The reduction half-reaction is:
$Mg^{2+} + 2e^- \rightarrow Mg(s)$

This equation is crucial because it tells us the stoichiometry: **2 moles of electrons are required to produce 1 mole of magnesium.**

### Step 2: Calculate the Total Electric Charge (Q)

The total charge passed through the melt can be calculated using the formula:
**Charge (Q) = Current (I) × Time (t)**

First, we need to ensure our units are consistent. The unit of current, Ampere (A), is Coulombs per second (C/s). Therefore, we must convert the time from hours to seconds.

*   **Current (I):** 100,000 A = 100,000 C/s
*   **Time (t):** 1.00 h × (60 min / 1 h) × (60 s / 1 min) = 3600 s

Now, calculate the charge (Q):
$Q = (100,000 \text{ C/s}) \times (3600 \text{ s}) = 3.60 \times 10^8 \text{ C}$

### Step 3: Calculate the Moles of Electrons

Faraday's constant ($F$) relates the charge in Coulombs to the moles of electrons.
**$F = 96,485 \text{ C/mol } e^-$**

We can find the total moles of electrons ($n_{e^-}$) that were passed through the cell:
$n_{e^-} = \frac{\text{Total Charge (Q)}}{\text{Faraday's Constant (F)}} = \frac{3.60 \times 10^8 \text{ C}}{96,485 \text{ C/mol } e^-} = 3731.15 \text{ mol } e^-$

### Step 4: Calculate the Theoretical Moles and Mass of Magnesium

Using the stoichiometry from Step 1 (2 mol e⁻ produces 1 mol Mg), we can find the theoretical moles of magnesium that can be formed.

$ \text{moles of Mg} = 3731.15 \text{ mol } e^- \times (\frac{1 \text{ mol Mg}}{2 \text{ mol } e^-}) = 1865.58 \text{ mol Mg} $

Now, convert these moles to a mass using the molar mass of magnesium (Molar Mass of Mg ≈ 24.305 g/mol). This will be the **theoretical yield**.

$ \text{Theoretical Mass} = 1865.58 \text{ mol Mg} \times 24.305 \text{ g/mol} = 45,322 \text{ g} $

### Step 5: Calculate the Actual Mass Based on the Yield

The problem states that the actual yield is 85% of the theoretical yield.

$ \text{Actual Yield} = \text{Theoretical Yield} \times \text{Percent Yield} $
$ \text{Actual Mass} = 45,322 \text{ g} \times 0.85 = 38,524 \text{ g} $

Since the initial values have three significant figures (1.00 h), we should round our final answer to three significant figures.

$ \text{Actual Mass} \approx 38,500 \text{ g} $

This is a large mass, so it's common to express it in kilograms.
$ 38,500 \text{ g} \times (\frac{1 \text{ kg}}{1000 \text{ g}}) = 38.5 \text{ kg} $

***

### Final Answer:

The mass of magnesium that forms is **38.5 kg**.

### Solving the Problem: Silicon (Si)

**Step 1: Locate Silicon on the Periodic Table**

1.  Find **Silicon (Si)** on the periodic table.
2.  It is in **Period 3** (the 3rd row). This tells us its valence (outermost) electrons are in the n=3 principal energy level.
3.  It is in **Group 14** (or IVA).
4.  Most importantly, find its **atomic number (Z)**, which is **14**. For a neutral silicon atom, the atomic number equals the number of protons and also the number of electrons. Therefore, we need to place 14 electrons into orbitals.

**Step 2: Fill the Orbitals in Order of Energy (Aufbau Principle)**

We can use the structure of the periodic table to guide us through filling the orbitals from lowest energy to highest.

1.  **Period 1 (n=1):** This row corresponds to the **1s** orbital. It has two elements (H and He), so we fill the 1s orbital with 2 electrons.
    *   Configuration: `1s²`
    *   Electrons placed: 2. (12 remaining)

2.  **Period 2 (n=2):**
    *   The first two elements (Li, Be) are in the s-block. We fill the **2s** orbital with 2 electrons.
        *   Configuration: `1s² 2s²`
        *   Electrons placed: 4. (10 remaining)
    *   The next six elements (B through Ne) are in the p-block. We fill the **2p** orbitals with 6 electrons.
        *   Configuration: `1s² 2s² 2p⁶`
        *   Electrons placed: 10. (4 remaining)

3.  **Period 3 (n=3):** This is silicon's period.
    *   The first two elements (Na, Mg) are in the s-block. We fill the **3s** orbital with 2 electrons.
        *   Configuration: `1s² 2s² 2p⁶ 3s²`
        *   Electrons placed: 12. (2 remaining)
    *   Silicon is the second element in the p-block of this period. This means we place the final 2 electrons into the **3p** orbitals.
        *   Final Configuration: `1s² 2s² 2p⁶ 3s² 3p²`

**Step 3: Include Empty Orbitals in the Valence Shell**

The question specifically asks for the empty orbitals *in the valence shell*.

1.  **Identify the Valence Shell:** For silicon, the highest principal energy number (n) is 3. So, the valence shell is the **n=3 shell**.
2.  **Identify all orbitals in the n=3 shell:** The n=3 shell contains **3s**, **3p**, and **3d** sublevels.
3.  **Check their occupancy:**
    *   `3s²`: The 3s orbital is full.
    *   `3p²`: The 3p sublevel consists of three orbitals ($3p_x, 3p_y, 3p_z$). According to Hund's Rule, the 2 electrons will occupy two different 3p orbitals, leaving **one 3p orbital empty**.
    *   `3d`: The 3d sublevel exists in the n=3 shell, but for silicon, it contains no electrons. Therefore, all **five 3d orbitals are empty**.

To write the complete configuration including these empty orbitals, we can represent them with a superscript of zero.

***

### Final Answer

The complete electron configuration for silicon, including the empty orbitals in its valence shell, is:

**1s² 2s² 2p⁶ 3s² 3p² 3d⁰**

Question: Describe the hybridization of silicon and the molecular structure of the following molecules and ions: (a) (CH_3)_3SiH (b) $$\text{SiO}_{4} ^{4-}$$ (c) Si_2H_6 (d) Si(OH)_4 (e) $$\text{SiF}_{6} ^{2-}$$
Answer: (a) (CH_3)_3SiH: sp ^3 bonding about Si; the structure is tetrahedral; (b) $$\text{SiO}_{4} ^{\text{4unicode[:-]}} :$$ sp ^3 bonding about Si; the structure is tetrahedral; (c) Si_2H_6: sp ^3 bonding about each Si; the structure is linear along the Si-Si bond; (d) Si(OH)_4: sp ^3 bonding about Si; the structure is tetrahedral; (e) $$\text{SiF}_{6} ^{\text{2unicode[:-]}} :$$ sp ^3 d ^2 bonding about Si; the structure is octahedral
o3 Answer:
For each species count the σ-electron pairs that are attached to the silicon atom(s).  
The “steric number’’ ( σ-bonds + lone-pairs) fixes the hybridization and therefore the molecular
geometry that surrounds each Si centre.

a) (CH3)3SiH   (trimethyl­silane)  
Bonds at Si: 3 × Si–C + 1 × Si–H = 4 σ-bonds, no lone pair → steric number 4  
Hybridization of Si: sp3  
Local geometry: tetrahedral (C–Si–C and C–Si–H angles ≈ 109.5°).  
Overall molecule is the Si analogue of CH4 with the three H atoms replaced by CH3 groups.

b) SiO4^4–   (orthosilicate tetrahedron)  
Bonds at Si: 4 × Si–O single bonds, no lone pair → steric number 4  
Hybridization of Si: sp3  
Geometry: perfect tetrahedron; the four O atoms occupy the corners (O–Si–O ≈ 109.5°).

c) Si2H6   (disilane, H3Si–SiH3)  
Each Si is attached to 3 H atoms + the other Si atom ⇒ 4 σ-bonds, no lone pair  
Hybridization of each Si: sp3  
Geometry about each Si: tetrahedral.  
The whole molecule is the silicon analogue of ethane with a staggered or eclipsed Si–Si
conformation; there are no bridging hydrogens.

d) Si(OH)4   (orthosilicic acid)  
Bonds at Si: 4 × Si–O (each O bears an H) ⇒ steric number 4  
Hybridization of Si: sp3  
Geometry: tetrahedral arrangement of the four oxygen atoms around silicon
( O–Si–O ≈ 109.5°); each O–H bond is bent at the oxygen.

e) SiF6^2–   (hexafluoro­silicate ion)  
Bonds at Si: 6 × Si–F; no lone pair ⇒ steric number 6  
Hybridization of Si: sp3d2  
Geometry: octahedral; the six F atoms occupy the vertices of a regular octahedron
(F–Si–F angles 90°/180°).

Summary  
(a) sp3, tetrahedral (b) sp3, tetrahedral (c) sp3, tetrahedral (about each Si)  
(d) sp3, tetrahedral (e) sp3d2, octahedral

Silicon (Si) is in Group 14 of the periodic table, just below carbon. It has four valence electrons ($[Ne] 3s^2 3p^2$). This allows silicon to form four covalent bonds, similar to carbon.

5.  **Molecular Structure:** With six bonding atoms and no lone pairs, the electron geometry and the molecular structure are both **octahedral**. The F-Si-F bond angles are 90° and 180°.

Question: Classify each of the following molecules as polar or nonpolar. You may wish to review the chapter on chemical bonding. (a) SiH_4 (b) Si_2H_6 (c) SiCl_3H (d) SiF_4 (e) SiCl_2F_2

o3 Answer:
To decide whether a molecule is polar or non-polar we need two things:

1. Molecular shape (from VSEPR).  
2. Whether the bond dipoles can cancel in that shape.

For all of the species below the central atom(s) are tetrahedral (sp³).

a) SiH₄  
Tetrahedral with four identical Si–H bonds.  All bond dipoles are equivalent and point toward the corners of a regular tetrahedron, so they cancel completely.  
⇒ non-polar

b) Si₂H₆  (disilane)  
Each Si is tetrahedral (three Si–H bonds and one Si–Si bond).  
• The three Si–H bonds on one Si give a small net dipole that points along the Si–Si axis, but the second Si has an equal dipole in the opposite direction.  
• Free rotation about the Si–Si bond means the two dipoles cancel on average.  
⇒ non-polar  (analogous to ethane, C₂H₆)

c) SiCl₃H  
Tetrahedral with three highly polar Si–Cl bonds and one much less polar Si–H bond.  Because one corner is different from the other three, the bond dipoles do not cancel.  
⇒ polar

d) SiF₄  
Tetrahedral with four identical Si–F bonds.  A perfect tetrahedron with identical bonds has complete cancellation of dipoles.  
⇒ non-polar

e) SiCl₂F₂  
Tetrahedral with two Si–Cl and two Si–F bonds.  The two kinds of bonds have different magnitudes of dipole moment, and in a tetrahedron no pair of vertices is directly opposite the other; thus the dipoles cannot cancel completely.  (Dichlorodifluoromethane, CCl₂F₂, is a familiar polar analogue.)  
⇒ polar

Summary  
(a) SiH₄ – non-polar  
(b) Si₂H₆ – non-polar  
(c) SiCl₃H – polar  
(d) SiF₄ – non-polar  
(e) SiCl₂F₂ – polar

2.  **Molecular Geometry (Symmetry):** How are these polar bonds arranged in 3D space?
